original_reasoning: سقط 'fall/drop' aligns closely with a dripped or distilled substance like στακτή.
نارت=perfumery plant; νάρτη=plant used in perfumery. Strong direct match

Arabic الصندل = sandalwood (fragrant medicinal wood used for medicine and perfume). Greek σάνδαλον = sandal (footwear). Both are loanwords from the same Sanskrit/Old Iranian source (cf. Sanskrit चन्दन candana → Persian چندل → Arabic/Greek). The phonology matches perfectly (ṣ-n-d-l / s-n-d-l); they are co-borrowed siblings from the same Indic-Persian source lexeme.

Arabic القلف = bark/peel/husk; pomegranate rind; tree bark; foreskin (uncircumcised). Greek κέλυφος = sheath, case, shell, husk, pod. Both directly mean the outer shell/husk/case of something — Arabic القلف = to peel/shell; Greek κέλυφος = a shell/husk. Strong masadiq direct match through the outer-covering/peel/shell domain.

Arabic دلنظ (الدَلَنْظى): fat/plump person (heavy body). Greek σάνδαλον: sandal (footwear). Per calibration reference اثم→Θέμις=0.65; دلنظ→σάνδαλον shares phonological skeleton; both Semitic/Greek borrowing candidates.
حط is to put down or lower, directly matching barley poured down in ritual.
Greek means 'Aeolic' and Arabic explicitly mentions a language/dialect, giving a direct match in the domain of speech varieties.
حطا here is strong palm-grasping or enclosing; εἴχετε from ἔχω is to have or hold, a strong direct match.
